content.content:
1	---
2	name: fractal
3	description: Spawn and manage child nodes -- recursive autonomous agent loops.
4	---
5	
6	# Fractal
7	
8	A fractal is a tree of autonomous loops, each running in an isolated Git
9	worktree. You are one node; spawn a child to own a subtask that is well-defined,
10	separable, large enough for its own iteration cycle, and able to be run in
11	parallel without conflicting with your work. Pay careful attention to your
12	NODE.md instructions to guide you on how and when to spawn sub-nodes.
13	
14	If `$MAX_DEPTH`, `$MAX_CHILDREN`, or `$MAX_DESCENDANTS` is `0`, you are a leaf
15	-- you cannot spawn, so this skill (spawning and managing children) does not
16	apply; skip it and focus on executing your task directly. Otherwise, **default
17	to spawning.** If a task has separable parts, decompose it into children rather
18	than doing it yourself -- the fractal's power is multiplicative parallelism --
19	don't waste it by tackling tasks one at a time.
20	
21	Don't spawn what you can easily and reliably finish yourself. However, when a
22	child's task is itself complex, be a manager: give it resources and detailed
23	instructions on how to decompose further. Deep trees of focused nodes outperform
24	shallow trees of overloaded ones.
25	
26	Run `fractal node --help` and `fractal node <command> --help` for the CLI.
27	
28	## Limits (check before spawning)
29	
30	| Var                | Meaning                                                                     |
31	| ------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
32	| `$MAX_DEPTH`       | Maximum nesting depth below this node. `-1` unlimited, `0` leaf.            |
33	| `$MAX_CHILDREN`    | Maximum direct children of this node. `-1` unlimited, `0` leaf.             |
34	| `$MAX_DESCENDANTS` | Maximum total descendants in this node's subtree. `-1` unlimited, `0` leaf. |
35	
36	- **Width (`--max-children`).** Caps **direct** children only (not
37	  grandchildren). Enforced locally on the spawning node. A child may set a
38	  larger `--max-children` than its parent.
39	- **Depth (`--max-depth`) and descendants (`--max-descendants`).** Enforced
40	  across the **entire ancestor chain** -- `fractal node init` checks every
41	  ancestor's config and rejects the spawn if any limit would be exceeded. You do
42	  not need to split or decrement budgets when spawning; set whatever limits make
43	  sense for the child's subtask and let init enforce the ancestors' caps. A
44	  spawn that would breach any ancestor's cap fails fast with an error naming the
45	  offending node, so glance at `$MAX_DEPTH` and `$MAX_DESCENDANTS` before
46	  spawning to avoid wasted failed inits.
47	- **Re-entry re-checks.** Width and descendant caps count children in play --
48	  active, or idle awaiting start -- so a completed, stopped, exited, killed, or
49	  retired child frees its slot. `node start --continue` and a `node unretire`
50	  that lands `idle` put a node back in play and re-check both caps exactly like
51	  a spawn (refused over cap, no override); depth is structural, so only spawn
52	  checks it.
53	
54	## Cost
55	
56	A `--max-cost` (per-run USD ceiling -- runs are isolated, and a budget-ended run
57	refuses `node start --continue` without an explicit new `--max-cost`) is
58	strongly recommended for every child -- without one the child launches uncapped,
59	with a loud warning at start and bounded only by `--max-iters`/`--timeout`; a
60	non-positive `--max-cost` is rejected. Set it at init (`--max-cost`, \<= your
61	remaining when `$COST_BUDGET` is finite) and optionally `--max-iter-cost`.
62	Allocate conservatively; you may over-allocate across children optimistically,
63	but monitor (`fractal node cost spent`, `fractal node cost breakdown`) and kill
64	over-spenders. Caps are **soft** -- a child is not *hard*-stopped when it nears
65	`--max-cost`; once it drains into the reserve it gets cleanup guidance to wind
66	down the remainder of the current iteration cheaply, then the loop ends its run
67	at that iteration's boundary (the child runs `finish` itself only when its
68	requirements already verify -- the reserve guidance's goal-met exception) -- but
69	a single iteration or its subtree can still overshoot, so reining in an
70	over-spender is on you, the parent. Price a cap as solve + wind-down + reserve:
71	a clean finish costs a full iteration of close overhead on top of the last unit
72	of work (typically a few dollars per leaf child -- environment- and
73	model-specific), so a cap sized to the solve alone strands a *done* child
74	`exited` at the reserve boundary instead of `completed` unless it finishes
75	deliberately in the wind-down. Size `--max-iter-cost` to one wind-down pass as
76	well: the iteration cap, not the run-level cap, is what bounds each close
77	attempt. Iteration caps bind at step boundaries, never mid-step -- plan each
78	iteration to end under the cap, and read a trip as disclosure that an iteration
79	ran hot, not as a hard stop. If a done child strands anyway, prefer raise-cap +
80	continue + stop over a full re-finish -- the re-finish re-pays the whole close
81	severalfold and can still near-miss -- and treat `exited` with recorded DB
82	finish signals as a defensible terminal once the work is merged and verified.
83	Child-side corollary: `fractal node cost remaining` reports against
84	`--max-cost`, NOT against (`--max-cost` - reserve); a node inside the reserve
85	band reads positive remaining and plans an iteration the loop will never grant.
86	Plan your last productive iteration against (`--max-cost` - reserve), and if you
87	intend to signal `finish`, fire it BEFORE draining into the reserve -- wind-down
88	work scheduled for "next iteration" does not happen. In-step overshoot is
89	bounded for claude children (each step runs under a hard per-invocation budget
90	and stops cleanly at it), but the run-level cap stays soft. A child's spend
91	(including its sync) counts against your own budget. Some agents report cost
92	directly; others report token usage, priced from published rates -- so a cost
93	cap on a token-reporting child requires a (priced) `--model` (the run fails on
94	the first step otherwise). claude, grok, opencode, and omp report cost directly;
95	codex reports tokens. A claude child routed through openrouter is priced from
96	tokens too, so under a cap it also needs an explicit priced `--model`.
97	
98	Every dollar figure you read has a scope; know it before comparing two.
99	Budget-guard figures, recorded run-end reasons, and `fractal node cost spent` /
100	`cost remaining` are all full-depth **per-run subtree** spend -- the run's own
101	steps plus every descendant run chained under it -- so a spawning node's guard
102	trips on money its children spent. `fractal node activity`'s `cost` column is
103	the row's **own-node** step cost only (run and iteration end rows sum just that
104	node's steps), so activity totals read *below* the guard figures on a spawning
105	node by design -- the gap is the children's spend, itemized by `cost breakdown`.
106	And every figure is per-run: runs are isolated, and no cost command reports a
107	lifetime rollup -- reconcile across runs by scoping each run
108	(`cost spent --run <id>`) and summing.
109	
110	> [!WARNING]
111	> A **small `--max-cost` on a child running an expensive `--model`** is the
112	> combination most likely to blow the budget by a large *percentage*. The
113	> run-level cap is **soft** and only checked *between* steps, so one pricey step
114	> can be a big fraction of -- or exceed -- the child's whole budget before the
115	> next check. Give expensive-model children a budget large enough that a single
116	> step is a small slice, or hand a small budget to a cheaper `--model`. The
117	> sizing floor: never set `--max-cost` (or a remaining grant) within ~2x the
118	> model's single-turn cost -- a cap inside that band can be overshot by a large
119	> fraction in one turn, and that overshoot is documented, accepted behavior: no
120	> enforcement absorbs it.
121	
122	For well-specified single-mission leaf work, a cheaper model at the same dollar
123	cap (e.g. `--model=sonnet`) is a first-class choice -- cost-per-point favors it
124	on numeric, single-task work -- but give it iter-cap headroom: micro caps bind
125	on step granularity, and a fast drafter can pack a full iteration into one large
126	step. Keep frontier models for manager, audit, long-horizon, and judgment-call
127	roles: a cheaper model can hold process hygiene and mechanical output while its
128	judgment quality collapses -- at no cost saving, since under a binding budget
129	pool a cheaper token rate buys more steps, not a lower bill.
130	
131	## Spawn
132	
133	1. **Init:** run `fractal node init --help` to see available options, then run
134	   `fractal node init <name> --path="$PROJECT_DIR" [...]`. `--agent` is optional
135	   (currently `claude`, `codex`, `grok`, `opencode`, or `omp`): when omitted,
136	   the child automatically inherits your agent -- pass it only to give a child a
137	   different agent. `--provider` is optional the same way (e.g. `openrouter`
138	   routes claude or codex through OpenRouter; it inherits, and agents without
139	   routes ignore it). `--inherit=<surfaces>` (comma-separated: `steps`,
140	   `scripts`, `skills`, `config`, or `all`) seeds those surfaces from YOUR node
141	   dir instead of the package seed -- steps/scripts/skills copy your live files,
142	   `config` snapshots preference keys (model, effort, sync, detached, iter/step
143	   timing, retries, pacing) and never budget caps; agent config always inherits.
144	   `<name>` uses letters, digits, and `_` only (no `-`). **All run parameters**
145	   (budget, depth/children, iters, timing) are set here and stored in the
146	   child's `config.json` -- editable before launch. Capture the output for the
147	   child's project/node dirs. Init is lockfile-serialized -- run calls
148	   sequentially. A worked example of spawn hygiene -- every cap, the model, and
149	   the step timeout explicit at init (leaf caps shown -- give a manager child
150	   depth/children/descendants; fill every placeholder deliberately -- price the
151	   `<usd>` caps per Cost above, and when the tree's NODE.md names exact model
152	   recipes, they override this generic guidance), then a registry verify before
153	   any configuration; the init call is wrapped to capture the child worktree
154	   path from the init output's `Initialized <path>` line -- an unset
155	   `$child_worktree` silently reads YOUR OWN config (`--path=` resolves to the
156	   current directory; `--path` on `config get` names the child's worktree, not
157	   the project root):
158	
159	   ```bash
160	   child_worktree="$(fractal node init sub_a --path="$PROJECT_DIR" \
161	       --agent=<agent> --model=<explicit_model> \
162	       --max-cost=<usd> --max-iter-cost=<usd> \
163	       --max-iters=<n> --step-timeout=<duration> \
164	       --max-depth=0 --max-children=0 --max-descendants=0 \
165	       | sed -n 's/^Initialized //p')"
166	   fractal node list                                # registry row: sub_a present
167	   fractal node config get max_cost --path="$child_worktree"   # <usd> -- caps landed
168	   fractal node config get model --path="$child_worktree"      # <explicit_model>
169	   ```
170	
171	2. **Configure:** edit the child's `NODE.md` (instructions + completion
172	   requirements), `steps/`, `setup.sh`/`test.sh`/`lint.sh`, and `skills/` --
173	   invest here, it's the highest-leverage work (you can still steer after
174	   launch; see Configure below).
175	
176	   For a well-specified single-mission leaf, consider the **leaf step profile**:
177	   before the config commit, trim the child's `steps/` to PLAN + EXECUTE +
178	   COMMIT (delete the PREPARE and REVIEW files -- the loop runs whatever
179	   `steps/` contains, in digit-prefix order). Loop overhead dominates
180	   single-mission leaf cost, and a trimmed profile cuts it -- expect the
181	   overhead cut, not an outcome guarantee. Two caveats: PREPARE is where a node
182	   merges its parent and children -- keep it for any node that spawns, and for
183	   leaves whose upstream moves mid-run -- when you cannot rule that out at spawn
184	   time, keep PREPARE; REVIEW is where memory folds -- when you trim it,
185	   relocate its duties into a surviving step (e.g. append the fold to EXECUTE's
186	   tail) or, minimally, tell the child in its NODE.md to fold memory before
187	   COMMIT. Managers keep the full profile. When your own `steps/` already fits
188	   the child, skip the hand-trim and pass `--inherit=steps` at init.
189	
190	3. **Commit the config** so the child starts from a committed baseline (a
191	   continue refuses over uncommitted project files -- commit them or pass
192	   `--clean` to discard them). Run the commit **from the child's worktree** -- a
193	   bare `commit` acts on the current directory's worktree (yours), not the
194	   child's (or pass `--path=<child worktree>`):
195	
196	   ```bash
197	   cd <child worktree>  # .worktrees/<branch>
198	   fractal commit "configure <name>" --init
199	   ```
200	
201	   Material you copy into a child's tree passes through the repo's commit hooks
202	   (if any) at this init commit (CRLF->LF, formatter reflow) -- so a byte-frozen
203	   store and its delivered copies diverge *by construction* on the first hooked
204	   commit; do not read that divergence as tamper. If you freeze reference bytes
205	   for audit, freeze the post-hook form (commit once, then re-pin) or compare
206	   normalized (line endings/whitespace) rather than raw.
207	
208	4. **Launch:**
209	
210	   ```bash
211	   fractal node start <branch>
212	   ```
213	
214	   `start` takes no config arguments -- all run parameters come from
215	   `config.json` (set at init; adjust a value with
216	   `fractal node config set <key>=<value>`, read one with
217	   `fractal node config get <key>`, or edit the file directly). A configured
218	   `max_cost` must be positive; a missing `max_cost` launches uncapped with a
219	   loud warning. Add `--continue` only to continue a stopped/exited child.
220	   Starting is its own turn: when a spawn gate (child/descendant census, budget
221	   arithmetic) decides the launch, read it in one command and start in a
222	   separate one -- a chained start commits before you can see the read's output.
223	   The init gate re-checks census and budget at start, so treat a rejected start
224	   as the gate working; re-read before retrying.
225	
226	### Configure
227	
228	Node configuration is the highest-leverage work you do -- a well-configured node
229	runs autonomously for hours; a poorly configured one burns budget and creates
230	entropy. Invest real time here, and commit the baseline before launch -- a
231	continue refuses over uncommitted project files until they are committed or
232	discarded with `--clean`. You can still steer a running child by editing its
233	NODE.md, steps, or scripts (the loop re-reads them each iteration), but a strong
234	baseline is fundamental.
235	
236	For complex tasks, configure nodes as a manager: provide sufficient resources
237	for them to spawn their own children, and write detailed NODE.md instructions to
238	direct decomposition.
239	
240	- **NODE.md** (`<child_node_dir>/NODE.md`): The child reads this fresh each
241	  step, so it is both the initial brief and the live steering channel. Write
242	  clear instructions, completion requirements, and add any relevant constraints.
243	  You can edit it after launch to redirect the child mid-run.
244	- **Steps** (`steps/`): Don't change your own steps, but when configuring a
245	  child you may add or replace step files (the loop re-discovers them each
246	  iteration) to fit the task -- e.g. adversarial plan/review/critic steps,
247	  dedicated research or test/debugging steps, or multi-pass execution, etc. If
248	  sync is enabled (the default), it runs automatically before each step to
249	  handle radio communication. The first and last steps (PREPARE and COMMIT in
250	  the stock set) are structurally important to the lifecycle (merging parent
251	  changes, committing work) -- do not remove or fundamentally alter them (one
252	  scoped exception: the leaf step profile in Spawn step 2 drops PREPARE for a
253	  childless leaf whose upstream won't move mid-run). Middle steps can be freely
254	  renamed, added, or replaced. A step file may begin with YAML frontmatter:
255	  `agent: <command>` runs it on a different agent (each agent keeps its own
256	  woven session across the steps it runs), `provider: <route>` overrides the
257	  provider route (agents without routes ignore it), `model: <name>` overrides
258	  the model, `effort: <level>` overrides the reasoning effort,
259	  `timeout: <duration>` overrides the node-global `step_timeout` for that step
260	  alone, `detached: true` isolates that step in its own session within a
261	  continuous node, and `requires_approval: true` holds the loop after the step
262	  completes until you approve it (`fractal node pending`/`approve`). Size step
263	  timeouts where the step is defined -- give a slow test or research step its
264	  own `timeout:` ceiling rather than inflating the global -- and retune the
265	  global mid-run with `fractal node update --step-timeout` (it lands at the next
266	  iteration top). Set a generous global `step_timeout` too, as a resilience
267	  floor: without one a hung or stalled invocation (a stuck API call, a wedged
268	  tool) blocks the step and the whole node indefinitely, where a ceiling lets
269	  the loop abort and retry. Pass `--no-sync` at init to disable sync for
270	  lightweight leaf nodes.
271	- **Scripts** (`setup.sh`/`test.sh`/`lint.sh`): `setup.sh` runs every iteration
272	  (keep it idempotent) -- add dependency installs, env setup, or data seeding
273	  here. `lint.sh` is invoked by `fractal commit` and `test.sh` during EXECUTE;
274	  extend them to match the child's scope (e.g. narrower test paths, additional
275	  linters). A parent's tuned scripts propagate with `--inherit=scripts` at init.
276	- **Skills** (`skills/`): add or customize skill files to give the child
277	  domain-specific capabilities beyond the defaults.
278	
279	The seed lives in the node data directory (`.fractal/`), which is **tracked by
280	git** and captured by your `fractal commit --init`; the same goes for the
281	project wiki (`wiki/`). Neither belongs in `.gitignore`. Fractal ignores its own
282	runtime artifacts (worktrees, the central database, status, agent logs) via the
283	repo-local `.git/info/exclude`, which it writes automatically.
284	
285	### Meta nodes
286	
287	A meta node configures another node's seed instead of doing project work
288	directly. Use `--meta=<target_branch>` at init to create one -- this sets
289	`--base` to the target's branch and `--scope` to its seed directory
290	(`.fractal/<target-branch>`), so the meta node can only edit the target's seed
291	files (NODE.md, steps, scripts, skills, etc.). `$META_MODE` is `true` when
292	running as a meta node, and `$META_TARGET` is the target node's branch.
293	
294	Use a meta node when a child's configuration is complex enough to warrant its
295	own iteration cycle. The meta node studies the project and writes a high-quality
296	seed; once done, merge it and launch the target.
297	
298	## Monitor and control
299	
300	- **Status:** `fractal node list`, `fractal node status <branch>`. A node whose
301	  run ends on its `--max-cost` reports `exited` (exit 0 -- a designed landing,
302	  not a crash) -- a deliberate under-claim (a budget-*aborted* node must never
303	  read `completed`). The one exception: a node whose requirements verified and
304	  that ran a deliberate goal-met `finish` books `completed` even when the drain
305	  crossed the cap, with the overshoot recorded on the run row. So treat a capped
306	  node's `exited` as "inspect the work", not "failed": check its memory/plans
307	  and merge if the work is done. Verify a child's *executable* artifacts --
308	  checkers, scripts, anything you will run -- from a clean checkout of its
309	  committed bytes
310	  (`tmp=$(mktemp -d) && git archive <branch> | tar -xf - -C "$tmp"`), never
311	  in-place in a working tree: artifacts that depend on uncommitted or
312	  path-relative state pass in-place and fail everywhere else. (Textual claims
313	  need only `git show <branch>:<path>`.)
314	- **Stop:** `fractal node finish <branch>` (after iteration),
315	  `fractal node stop <branch>` (after step), `fractal node kill <branch>`
316	  (immediately).
317	- **Pause:** `fractal node pause <branch>` freezes the child's subtree in place
318	  (aborts in-flight agent turns; loops park with their runs open),
319	  `fractal node resume <branch>` relaunches it exactly there -- same budgets,
320	  same iteration count. A paused child still holds its spawn slot and blocks
321	  your finish-drain, so resume or kill it before finishing. Only `resume`,
322	  `kill`, and `chat` act on a paused node; `start --continue` is for
323	  stopped/exited nodes (fresh run, restored worktree -- uncommitted project
324	  files need `--clean`, and a budget-ended run needs an explicit `--max-cost`),
325	  never for paused ones.
326	- **Clean up:** `fractal node merge <branch>`. Deleting after the merge
327	  (`fractal node delete <branch>`) is OPTIONAL hygiene, never automatic -- a
328	  merged node's branch and records keep audit value, so keep them unless clutter
329	  demands otherwise. Delete is destructive -- it force-removes the worktree and
330	  force-deletes the branch (and the whole subtree) regardless of merge state,
331	  discarding any unmerged work, so confirm the merge succeeded first.
332	
333	## Continue mode
334	
335	When `$CONTINUE_MODE` is `true`, decide per child whether to propagate by
336	assessing its memory/plans:
337	
338	- **continue** (`fractal node start <branch> --continue`) if its work was in
339	  progress and still relevant -- uncommitted project files in its worktree
340	  refuse the launch, so commit them or add `--clean` to discard them;
341	- **reset** (`fractal node init <name> --path="$PROJECT_DIR" --reset`, then
342	  start) if the direction was wrong but the task stands -- `--reset` wipes the
343	  node to a **stock empty node** (memory, plans, steps, skills, config all
344	  cleared), so you must re-author its NODE.md, steps, and skills before
345	  starting;
346	- **delete** (`fractal node delete <branch>`) if the task is no longer needed;
347	  or
348	- **leave** (merge its work if `completed` and not yet merged).
349	
350	## Radio
351	
352	Every node has a radio (auto-initialized) for live inter-node messaging --
353	channels `public`, `private` (owner-only), `inbox` (others write), `outbox` (you
354	write); you auto-subscribe to the readable channels (`public` and `outbox`) of
355	your parent and each direct child. If sync is enabled, radio is checked before
356	every step, so messages and directives are picked up automatically. It is the
357	live coordination path (the project wiki only syncs at merge). Messages have a
358	priority (0-10, higher = more urgent). Run `fractal radio --help` (and
359	`fractal radio <command> --help`) to explore the CLI. See the `radio` skill for
360	messaging conventions.
361	
362	Commands act on the current directory's node, so run them from your worktree --
363	you never pass a path for yourself. Name another node's branch positionally to
364	act on it (e.g. `fractal node status <branch>`); `--path` is only for running
365	from outside a worktree. `fractal node init` is the exception: `<name>` plus the
366	project root via `--path` (e.g. `$PROJECT_DIR`).
367

file.content:
---
name: fractal
description: Spawn and manage child nodes -- recursive autonomous agent loops.
---

# Fractal

A fractal is a tree of autonomous loops, each running in an isolated Git
worktree. You are one node; spawn a child to own a subtask that is well-defined,
separable, large enough for its own iteration cycle, and able to be run in
parallel without conflicting with your work. Pay careful attention to your
NODE.md instructions to guide you on how and when to spawn sub-nodes.

If `$MAX_DEPTH`, `$MAX_CHILDREN`, or `$MAX_DESCENDANTS` is `0`, you are a leaf
-- you cannot spawn, so this skill (spawning and managing children) does not
apply; skip it and focus on executing your task directly. Otherwise, **default
to spawning.** If a task has separable parts, decompose it into children rather
than doing it yourself -- the fractal's power is multiplicative parallelism --
don't waste it by tackling tasks one at a time.

Don't spawn what you can easily and reliably finish yourself. However, when a
child's task is itself complex, be a manager: give it resources and detailed
instructions on how to decompose further. Deep trees of focused nodes outperform
shallow trees of overloaded ones.

Run `fractal node --help` and `fractal node <command> --help` for the CLI.

## Limits (check before spawning)

| Var                | Meaning                                                                     |
| ------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `$MAX_DEPTH`       | Maximum nesting depth below this node. `-1` unlimited, `0` leaf.            |
| `$MAX_CHILDREN`    | Maximum direct children of this node. `-1` unlimited, `0` leaf.             |
| `$MAX_DESCENDANTS` | Maximum total descendants in this node's subtree. `-1` unlimited, `0` leaf. |

- **Width (`--max-children`).** Caps **direct** children only (not
  grandchildren). Enforced locally on the spawning node. A child may set a
  larger `--max-children` than its parent.
- **Depth (`--max-depth`) and descendants (`--max-descendants`).** Enforced
  across the **entire ancestor chain** -- `fractal node init` checks every
  ancestor's config and rejects the spawn if any limit would be exceeded. You do
  not need to split or decrement budgets when spawning; set whatever limits make
  sense for the child's subtask and let init enforce the ancestors' caps. A
  spawn that would breach any ancestor's cap fails fast with an error naming the
  offending node, so glance at `$MAX_DEPTH` and `$MAX_DESCENDANTS` before
  spawning to avoid wasted failed inits.
- **Re-entry re-checks.** Width and descendant caps count children in play --
  active, or idle awaiting start -- so a completed, stopped, exited, killed, or
  retired child frees its slot. `node start --continue` and a `node unretire`
  that lands `idle` put a node back in play and re-check both caps exactly like
  a spawn (refused over cap, no override); depth is structural, so only spawn
  checks it.

## Cost

A `--max-cost` (per-run USD ceiling -- runs are isolated, and a budget-ended run
refuses `node start --continue` without an explicit new `--max-cost`) is
strongly recommended for every child -- without one the child launches uncapped,
with a loud warning at start and bounded only by `--max-iters`/`--timeout`; a
non-positive `--max-cost` is rejected. Set it at init (`--max-cost`, \<= your
remaining when `$COST_BUDGET` is finite) and optionally `--max-iter-cost`.
Allocate conservatively; you may over-allocate across children optimistically,
but monitor (`fractal node cost spent`, `fractal node cost breakdown`) and kill
over-spenders. Caps are **soft** -- a child is not *hard*-stopped when it nears
`--max-cost`; once it drains into the reserve it gets cleanup guidance to wind
down the remainder of the current iteration cheaply, then the loop ends its run
at that iteration's boundary (the child runs `finish` itself only when its
requirements already verify -- the reserve guidance's goal-met exception) -- but
a single iteration or its subtree can still overshoot, so reining in an
over-spender is on you, the parent. Price a cap as solve + wind-down + reserve:
a clean finish costs a full iteration of close overhead on top of the last unit
of work (typically a few dollars per leaf child -- environment- and
model-specific), so a cap sized to the solve alone strands a *done* child
`exited` at the reserve boundary instead of `completed` unless it finishes
deliberately in the wind-down. Size `--max-iter-cost` to one wind-down pass as
well: the iteration cap, not the run-level cap, is what bounds each close
attempt. Iteration caps bind at step boundaries, never mid-step -- plan each
iteration to end under the cap, and read a trip as disclosure that an iteration
ran hot, not as a hard stop. If a done child strands anyway, prefer raise-cap +
continue + stop over a full re-finish -- the re-finish re-pays the whole close
severalfold and can still near-miss -- and treat `exited` with recorded DB
finish signals as a defensible terminal once the work is merged and verified.
Child-side corollary: `fractal node cost remaining` reports against
`--max-cost`, NOT against (`--max-cost` - reserve); a node inside the reserve
band reads positive remaining and plans an iteration the loop will never grant.
Plan your last productive iteration against (`--max-cost` - reserve), and if you
intend to signal `finish`, fire it BEFORE draining into the reserve -- wind-down
work scheduled for "next iteration" does not happen. In-step overshoot is
bounded for claude children (each step runs under a hard per-invocation budget
and stops cleanly at it), but the run-level cap stays soft. A child's spend
(including its sync) counts against your own budget. Some agents report cost
directly; others report token usage, priced from published rates -- so a cost
cap on a token-reporting child requires a (priced) `--model` (the run fails on
the first step otherwise). claude, grok, opencode, and omp report cost directly;
codex reports tokens. A claude child routed through openrouter is priced from
tokens too, so under a cap it also needs an explicit priced `--model`.

Every dollar figure you read has a scope; know it before comparing two.
Budget-guard figures, recorded run-end reasons, and `fractal node cost spent` /
`cost remaining` are all full-depth **per-run subtree** spend -- the run's own
steps plus every descendant run chained under it -- so a spawning node's guard
trips on money its children spent. `fractal node activity`'s `cost` column is
the row's **own-node** step cost only (run and iteration end rows sum just that
node's steps), so activity totals read *below* the guard figures on a spawning
node by design -- the gap is the children's spend, itemized by `cost breakdown`.
And every figure is per-run: runs are isolated, and no cost command reports a
lifetime rollup -- reconcile across runs by scoping each run
(`cost spent --run <id>`) and summing.

> [!WARNING]
> A **small `--max-cost` on a child running an expensive `--model`** is the
> combination most likely to blow the budget by a large *percentage*. The
> run-level cap is **soft** and only checked *between* steps, so one pricey step
> can be a big fraction of -- or exceed -- the child's whole budget before the
> next check. Give expensive-model children a budget large enough that a single
> step is a small slice, or hand a small budget to a cheaper `--model`. The
> sizing floor: never set `--max-cost` (or a remaining grant) within ~2x the
> model's single-turn cost -- a cap inside that band can be overshot by a large
> fraction in one turn, and that overshoot is documented, accepted behavior: no
> enforcement absorbs it.

For well-specified single-mission leaf work, a cheaper model at the same dollar
cap (e.g. `--model=sonnet`) is a first-class choice -- cost-per-point favors it
on numeric, single-task work -- but give it iter-cap headroom: micro caps bind
on step granularity, and a fast drafter can pack a full iteration into one large
step. Keep frontier models for manager, audit, long-horizon, and judgment-call
roles: a cheaper model can hold process hygiene and mechanical output while its
judgment quality collapses -- at no cost saving, since under a binding budget
pool a cheaper token rate buys more steps, not a lower bill.

## Spawn

1. **Init:** run `fractal node init --help` to see available options, then run
   `fractal node init <name> --path="$PROJECT_DIR" [...]`. `--agent` is optional
   (currently `claude`, `codex`, `grok`, `opencode`, or `omp`): when omitted,
   the child automatically inherits your agent -- pass it only to give a child a
   different agent. `--provider` is optional the same way (e.g. `openrouter`
   routes claude or codex through OpenRouter; it inherits, and agents without
   routes ignore it). `--inherit=<surfaces>` (comma-separated: `steps`,
   `scripts`, `skills`, `config`, or `all`) seeds those surfaces from YOUR node
   dir instead of the package seed -- steps/scripts/skills copy your live files,
   `config` snapshots preference keys (model, effort, sync, detached, iter/step
   timing, retries, pacing) and never budget caps; agent config always inherits.
   `<name>` uses letters, digits, and `_` only (no `-`). **All run parameters**
   (budget, depth/children, iters, timing) are set here and stored in the
   child's `config.json` -- editable before launch. Capture the output for the
   child's project/node dirs. Init is lockfile-serialized -- run calls
   sequentially. A worked example of spawn hygiene -- every cap, the model, and
   the step timeout explicit at init (leaf caps shown -- give a manager child
   depth/children/descendants; fill every placeholder deliberately -- price the
   `<usd>` caps per Cost above, and when the tree's NODE.md names exact model
   recipes, they override this generic guidance), then a registry verify before
   any configuration; the init call is wrapped to capture the child worktree
   path from the init output's `Initialized <path>` line -- an unset
   `$child_worktree` silently reads YOUR OWN config (`--path=` resolves to the
   current directory; `--path` on `config get` names the child's worktree, not
   the project root):

   ```bash
   child_worktree="$(fractal node init sub_a --path="$PROJECT_DIR" \
       --agent=<agent> --model=<explicit_model> \
       --max-cost=<usd> --max-iter-cost=<usd> \
       --max-iters=<n> --step-timeout=<duration> \
       --max-depth=0 --max-children=0 --max-descendants=0 \
       | sed -n 's/^Initialized //p')"
   fractal node list                                # registry row: sub_a present
   fractal node config get max_cost --path="$child_worktree"   # <usd> -- caps landed
   fractal node config get model --path="$child_worktree"      # <explicit_model>
   ```

2. **Configure:** edit the child's `NODE.md` (instructions + completion
   requirements), `steps/`, `setup.sh`/`test.sh`/`lint.sh`, and `skills/` --
   invest here, it's the highest-leverage work (you can still steer after
   launch; see Configure below).

   For a well-specified single-mission leaf, consider the **leaf step profile**:
   before the config commit, trim the child's `steps/` to PLAN + EXECUTE +
   COMMIT (delete the PREPARE and REVIEW files -- the loop runs whatever
   `steps/` contains, in digit-prefix order). Loop overhead dominates
   single-mission leaf cost, and a trimmed profile cuts it -- expect the
   overhead cut, not an outcome guarantee. Two caveats: PREPARE is where a node
   merges its parent and children -- keep it for any node that spawns, and for
   leaves whose upstream moves mid-run -- when you cannot rule that out at spawn
   time, keep PREPARE; REVIEW is where memory folds -- when you trim it,
   relocate its duties into a surviving step (e.g. append the fold to EXECUTE's
   tail) or, minimally, tell the child in its NODE.md to fold memory before
   COMMIT. Managers keep the full profile. When your own `steps/` already fits
   the child, skip the hand-trim and pass `--inherit=steps` at init.

3. **Commit the config** so the child starts from a committed baseline (a
   continue refuses over uncommitted project files -- commit them or pass
   `--clean` to discard them). Run the commit **from the child's worktree** -- a
   bare `commit` acts on the current directory's worktree (yours), not the
   child's (or pass `--path=<child worktree>`):

   ```bash
   cd <child worktree>  # .worktrees/<branch>
   fractal commit "configure <name>" --init
   ```

   Material you copy into a child's tree passes through the repo's commit hooks
   (if any) at this init commit (CRLF->LF, formatter reflow) -- so a byte-frozen
   store and its delivered copies diverge *by construction* on the first hooked
   commit; do not read that divergence as tamper. If you freeze reference bytes
   for audit, freeze the post-hook form (commit once, then re-pin) or compare
   normalized (line endings/whitespace) rather than raw.

4. **Launch:**

   ```bash
   fractal node start <branch>
   ```

   `start` takes no config arguments -- all run parameters come from
   `config.json` (set at init; adjust a value with
   `fractal node config set <key>=<value>`, read one with
   `fractal node config get <key>`, or edit the file directly). A configured
   `max_cost` must be positive; a missing `max_cost` launches uncapped with a
   loud warning. Add `--continue` only to continue a stopped/exited child.
   Starting is its own turn: when a spawn gate (child/descendant census, budget
   arithmetic) decides the launch, read it in one command and start in a
   separate one -- a chained start commits before you can see the read's output.
   The init gate re-checks census and budget at start, so treat a rejected start
   as the gate working; re-read before retrying.

### Configure

Node configuration is the highest-leverage work you do -- a well-configured node
runs autonomously for hours; a poorly configured one burns budget and creates
entropy. Invest real time here, and commit the baseline before launch -- a
continue refuses over uncommitted project files until they are committed or
discarded with `--clean`. You can still steer a running child by editing its
NODE.md, steps, or scripts (the loop re-reads them each iteration), but a strong
baseline is fundamental.

For complex tasks, configure nodes as a manager: provide sufficient resources
for them to spawn their own children, and write detailed NODE.md instructions to
direct decomposition.

- **NODE.md** (`<child_node_dir>/NODE.md`): The child reads this fresh each
  step, so it is both the initial brief and the live steering channel. Write
  clear instructions, completion requirements, and add any relevant constraints.
  You can edit it after launch to redirect the child mid-run.
- **Steps** (`steps/`): Don't change your own steps, but when configuring a
  child you may add or replace step files (the loop re-discovers them each
  iteration) to fit the task -- e.g. adversarial plan/review/critic steps,
  dedicated research or test/debugging steps, or multi-pass execution, etc. If
  sync is enabled (the default), it runs automatically before each step to
  handle radio communication. The first and last steps (PREPARE and COMMIT in
  the stock set) are structurally important to the lifecycle (merging parent
  changes, committing work) -- do not remove or fundamentally alter them (one
  scoped exception: the leaf step profile in Spawn step 2 drops PREPARE for a
  childless leaf whose upstream won't move mid-run). Middle steps can be freely
  renamed, added, or replaced. A step file may begin with YAML frontmatter:
  `agent: <command>` runs it on a different agent (each agent keeps its own
  woven session across the steps it runs), `provider: <route>` overrides the
  provider route (agents without routes ignore it), `model: <name>` overrides
  the model, `effort: <level>` overrides the reasoning effort,
  `timeout: <duration>` overrides the node-global `step_timeout` for that step
  alone, `detached: true` isolates that step in its own session within a
  continuous node, and `requires_approval: true` holds the loop after the step
  completes until you approve it (`fractal node pending`/`approve`). Size step
  timeouts where the step is defined -- give a slow test or research step its
  own `timeout:` ceiling rather than inflating the global -- and retune the
  global mid-run with `fractal node update --step-timeout` (it lands at the next
  iteration top). Set a generous global `step_timeout` too, as a resilience
  floor: without one a hung or stalled invocation (a stuck API call, a wedged
  tool) blocks the step and the whole node indefinitely, where a ceiling lets
  the loop abort and retry. Pass `--no-sync` at init to disable sync for
  lightweight leaf nodes.
- **Scripts** (`setup.sh`/`test.sh`/`lint.sh`): `setup.sh` runs every iteration
  (keep it idempotent) -- add dependency installs, env setup, or data seeding
  here. `lint.sh` is invoked by `fractal commit` and `test.sh` during EXECUTE;
  extend them to match the child's scope (e.g. narrower test paths, additional
  linters). A parent's tuned scripts propagate with `--inherit=scripts` at init.
- **Skills** (`skills/`): add or customize skill files to give the child
  domain-specific capabilities beyond the defaults.

The seed lives in the node data directory (`.fractal/`), which is **tracked by
git** and captured by your `fractal commit --init`; the same goes for the
project wiki (`wiki/`). Neither belongs in `.gitignore`. Fractal ignores its own
runtime artifacts (worktrees, the central database, status, agent logs) via the
repo-local `.git/info/exclude`, which it writes automatically.

### Meta nodes

A meta node configures another node's seed instead of doing project work
directly. Use `--meta=<target_branch>` at init to create one -- this sets
`--base` to the target's branch and `--scope` to its seed directory
(`.fractal/<target-branch>`), so the meta node can only edit the target's seed
files (NODE.md, steps, scripts, skills, etc.). `$META_MODE` is `true` when
running as a meta node, and `$META_TARGET` is the target node's branch.

Use a meta node when a child's configuration is complex enough to warrant its
own iteration cycle. The meta node studies the project and writes a high-quality
seed; once done, merge it and launch the target.

## Monitor and control

- **Status:** `fractal node list`, `fractal node status <branch>`. A node whose
  run ends on its `--max-cost` reports `exited` (exit 0 -- a designed landing,
  not a crash) -- a deliberate under-claim (a budget-*aborted* node must never
  read `completed`). The one exception: a node whose requirements verified and
  that ran a deliberate goal-met `finish` books `completed` even when the drain
  crossed the cap, with the overshoot recorded on the run row. So treat a capped
  node's `exited` as "inspect the work", not "failed": check its memory/plans
  and merge if the work is done. Verify a child's *executable* artifacts --
  checkers, scripts, anything you will run -- from a clean checkout of its
  committed bytes
  (`tmp=$(mktemp -d) && git archive <branch> | tar -xf - -C "$tmp"`), never
  in-place in a working tree: artifacts that depend on uncommitted or
  path-relative state pass in-place and fail everywhere else. (Textual claims
  need only `git show <branch>:<path>`.)
- **Stop:** `fractal node finish <branch>` (after iteration),
  `fractal node stop <branch>` (after step), `fractal node kill <branch>`
  (immediately).
- **Pause:** `fractal node pause <branch>` freezes the child's subtree in place
  (aborts in-flight agent turns; loops park with their runs open),
  `fractal node resume <branch>` relaunches it exactly there -- same budgets,
  same iteration count. A paused child still holds its spawn slot and blocks
  your finish-drain, so resume or kill it before finishing. Only `resume`,
  `kill`, and `chat` act on a paused node; `start --continue` is for
  stopped/exited nodes (fresh run, restored worktree -- uncommitted project
  files need `--clean`, and a budget-ended run needs an explicit `--max-cost`),
  never for paused ones.
- **Clean up:** `fractal node merge <branch>`. Deleting after the merge
  (`fractal node delete <branch>`) is OPTIONAL hygiene, never automatic -- a
  merged node's branch and records keep audit value, so keep them unless clutter
  demands otherwise. Delete is destructive -- it force-removes the worktree and
  force-deletes the branch (and the whole subtree) regardless of merge state,
  discarding any unmerged work, so confirm the merge succeeded first.

## Continue mode

When `$CONTINUE_MODE` is `true`, decide per child whether to propagate by
assessing its memory/plans:

- **continue** (`fractal node start <branch> --continue`) if its work was in
  progress and still relevant -- uncommitted project files in its worktree
  refuse the launch, so commit them or add `--clean` to discard them;
- **reset** (`fractal node init <name> --path="$PROJECT_DIR" --reset`, then
  start) if the direction was wrong but the task stands -- `--reset` wipes the
  node to a **stock empty node** (memory, plans, steps, skills, config all
  cleared), so you must re-author its NODE.md, steps, and skills before
  starting;
- **delete** (`fractal node delete <branch>`) if the task is no longer needed;
  or
- **leave** (merge its work if `completed` and not yet merged).

## Radio

Every node has a radio (auto-initialized) for live inter-node messaging --
channels `public`, `private` (owner-only), `inbox` (others write), `outbox` (you
write); you auto-subscribe to the readable channels (`public` and `outbox`) of
your parent and each direct child. If sync is enabled, radio is checked before
every step, so messages and directives are picked up automatically. It is the
live coordination path (the project wiki only syncs at merge). Messages have a
priority (0-10, higher = more urgent). Run `fractal radio --help` (and
`fractal radio <command> --help`) to explore the CLI. See the `radio` skill for
messaging conventions.

Commands act on the current directory's node, so run them from your worktree --
you never pass a path for yourself. Name another node's branch positionally to
act on it (e.g. `fractal node status <branch>`); `--path` is only for running
from outside a worktree. `fractal node init` is the exception: `<name>` plus the
project root via `--path` (e.g. `$PROJECT_DIR`).

input.content:
# Relay integration research: event-kind taxonomy

## Instructions

You are a research leaf under `main.platform_architect` (the Lindenmayer
platform architect). You produce ONE deliverable file you solely own:

**`docs/research/relay-integration/event-kinds.md`**

Do not create or edit any other file under `docs/` — in particular, never
touch `docs/DESIGN.md` (the architect owns it) or
`docs/research/relay-integration/degradation.md` (a sibling owns it) or its
`README.md` (the architect writes the aggregate). If you believe a contract
you were given is wrong, radio the architect (priority 6) instead of working
around it.

### Task

Design the event-kind taxonomy for Lindenmayer's relay integration: the
mapping from Lindenmayer telemetry to Nostr events. Ground truth you must
read first, from your own worktree:

- `docs/DESIGN.md` — especially §1: the adopted **Nostr-first layering**
  (Nostr core = standard NIPs + self-documented custom kinds, consumable by
  any compliant relay/client; Buzz layer = conveniences and relay-side
  behavior on top) and its **draft-dependency rule** (a Buzz NIP draft may be
  used by core only as schema-on-the-wire, never for relay-side enforcement).
- `docs/platforms.md` — the platform reference: Fractal's data model
  (§1.3–1.4: node/run/iter/step, costs, radio, approvals, events table) and
  Buzz's kind ranges and NIPs (§2.3–2.4: 40000–49999 custom range, NIP-29
  kinds, NIP-34 git kinds, NIP-AM/AO metric drafts).

Questions to answer, concretely:

1. **Telemetry inventory → mapping.** Enumerate what the bridge emits
   (node lifecycle, run/iter/step accounting, cost records, radio-derived
   aggregates, approval requests/verdicts, template registration/versioning,
   attestation) and for each: does it map onto a **standard NIP** (NIP-01
   notes, NIP-29 group messages, NIP-34 git events, NIP-25 reactions, etc.),
   or does it need a **custom kind**? Prefer standard NIPs where the
   semantics genuinely fit; do not shoehorn.
2. **Custom-kind design.** For each custom kind: proposed kind number range
   placement (Buzz's 40000–49999 custom convention vs the standard
   30000–39999 parameterized-replaceable range — reason about
   replaceable vs append-only semantics per event type; history/telemetry
   is generally append-only, registrations may be replaceable), tag schema
   sketch (a table per kind: tag, meaning, required?), and content payload
   shape.
3. **NIP-AM/AO alignment.** Where Buzz's metric drafts cover the same ground
   (agent turn metrics, observability), state how our kinds align and what
   we restate in our own docs so draft churn cannot orphan our history
   (per the draft-dependency rule).
4. **Documentation convention.** Propose how Lindenmayer documents its kinds
   for portability — e.g. a `docs/kinds/` NIP-style registry, one short spec
   per kind, versioned in-repo — so any Nostr client author can implement a
   reader without Buzz or Lindenmayer source access.

Constraints: this is a design-research document, not code. Respect the
design principles in DESIGN.md §6 (privacy: aggregates up, details stay in
the subgraph — kind design must not leak subgraph detail upward by default;
no new storage systems). Keep the document tight and decision-oriented:
recommendations with reasoning, not surveys. Aim for the concreteness a
future `core` node could implement from directly.

### Working style

- Structure the deliverable with an executive summary up top (the
  recommended taxonomy in one table), then per-kind detail.
- Bulk exploration notes go in your plans/memory, not the deliverable.
- Post progress to your outbox at priority 2–3; escalate blockers or
  contract friction to the architect's inbox at priority 6. Your parent is
  `main.platform_architect`.
- Your web access may be limited; `docs/platforms.md` plus its cited NIP
  names are sufficient ground truth. If you can fetch nostr-protocol/nips
  for exact kind-number tables, do; otherwise reason from platforms.md and
  say so in the document.
- Finish deliberately: when the deliverable is complete and committed, run
  `fractal node finish --reason="deliverable complete"` in that same
  iteration — do not wait for a next iteration or for parent sign-off.

## Completion Requirements

The run is complete when ALL hold:

- `docs/research/relay-integration/event-kinds.md` exists on your branch,
  committed, and answers all four questions above with concrete
  recommendations (kind mapping table, custom-kind sketches with tag
  schemas, NIP-AM/AO alignment statement, documentation convention).
- The file's own summary matches its delivered content (no advertised
  unwritten sections).
- A brief final note is posted to your outbox (priority 3) summarizing the
  recommended taxonomy in a few lines.

Then run `fractal node finish` in the same iteration.

1	You are an autonomous node iterating on a project in a git worktree.
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Paths:
6	
7	- Repo: $REPO_DIR

47	
48	<!-- Author the node's goals and directions here. -->
49	
50	## Completion Requirements
51	
52	<!-- Author the node's completion conditions here. -->
53	
54	## Rules
55	
56	- **Completion.** When all Completion Requirements are met, run
57	  `fractal node finish --reason="<reason>"` -- the way to signal your work is
58	  done while the node is running. Run it in the iteration that meets them: a
59	  finish deferred to a next iteration the budget may never grant leaves a done
60	  node `exited`, not `completed`. Until you do, the loop keeps iterating and
61	  spending budget. If that section is empty, never self-complete. When your
62	  Completion Requirements reference tests, run `bash $NODE_DIR/scripts/test.sh`
63	  and confirm it passes before finishing -- the loop never tests for you, so a
64	  `node finish` over failing tests books a false `completed`. Before
65	  `node finish`, drain in one pass: promote durable findings to the shared wiki
66	  (scrubbed of iteration labels) or post one outbox line stating why nothing
67	  promotes; prune memory to terminal state -- no forward-looking Remaining/NEXT
68	  lines; reconcile each document-of-record's title, intro, and abstract to
69	  DELIVERED content -- narrative surfaces must never advertise unwritten
70	  sections; and drain your saved radio queue (`messages --saved` -- unsave the
71	  done, act on or hand off the rest). Memory is yours; the wiki is what outlives
72	  you.
73	- **Memory (two-wiki doctrine).** TWO knowledge stores, different audiences.
74	  `$MEMORY_DIR` is the node's private brain -- what you don't write here, you
75	  won't remember next iteration. The project wiki (`$WIKI_DIR`) is the shared
76	  record other nodes reuse. Route each durable fact by audience (only future-you
77	  needs it -> memory; any other node -> wiki; a brief that bars the shared wiki
78	  routes everything to memory); don't duplicate a page across stores -- keep one
79	  canonical copy and point at it in plain text (wikilinks do not cross wikis).
80	  Read memory when you orient; fold durable findings back before the iteration
81	  ends. State pages -- status, orchestration, progress -- describe the work, not
82	  the timeline: no iteration labels anywhere in memory; say what stands, not
83	  when it landed.
84	- **Communication.** Radio is your voice -- your parent (auto-subscribed) and
85	  the user know only what you post. A silent node looks stuck and gets
86	  redirected or killed, so keep your outbox current with real progress,
87	  decisions, and blockers (not empty per-iteration noise). Surface anything the
88	  user needs and continue -- never wait on a reply. Radio is a two-way channel,
89	  not a broadcast log: read your inbox every iteration and REPLY to messages
90	  addressed to you (a question left unanswered stalls the asker); save a message
91	  that needs later action and unsave it when done; set priority by CONSEQUENCE
92	  -- a blocker or a decision the reader must act on is high, a status ping is
93	  low -- so the one message that matters is never drowned. Before escalating a
94	  claim about repo tooling or configuration as user action, verify it against
95	  the actual config or code and include the verification evidence in the message
96	  -- a confident misdiagnosis costs the reader more than the symptom.
97	- **Delegation.** When `$MAX_DEPTH`, `$MAX_CHILDREN`, and `$MAX_DESCENDANTS` are
98	  not `0`, you are a manager, not a laborer. Spawn a child when a trigger fires:
99	  a separable subtask with real depth of its own; independent subtasks that
100	  could run in parallel; a subtask that wants a clean context (long source
101	  material, or verification meant to be independent of whoever produced the
102	  work). Before spawning, price BOTH sides of the split: each child's cap covers
103	  its solve plus wind-down and reserve (a cap sized to the solve alone strands a
104	  done child `exited`, not `completed`; price a leaf's solve at no less than two
105	  full iterations of your own observed burn), and the children's caps, spawn
106	  ceremony, and one integration iteration must all fit inside YOUR remaining
107	  budget -- a stranded manager that cannot merge its children ships nothing, and
108	  sub-iteration chores stay yours. Size each child's form to its function: a
109	  narrow mechanical subtask gets a lighter `--model`, `--no-sync`, a trimmed
110	  step list (delete the seed steps it does not need before starting it), and a
111	  tight cap; the full synced cadence on a frontier model is for open-ended work
112	  with real unknowns -- spending it on a scoped edit is the manager's usage
113	  error, not the child's. Decide at PLAN time, out loud, against these triggers:
114	  solo work without citing a trigger and spawning for sub-iteration chores are
115	  the twin failure modes. Decompose into child nodes when your instructions
116	  direct it; when in doubt on a splittable task, *spawn*. The proven shape:
117	  `fractal commit` the shared skeleton and a frozen wiki interface contract
118	  first (a child forks your branch at its last commit, not your working tree --
119	  or inline what a child must read into its `NODE.md`), then give each child
120	  disjoint file ownership in its `NODE.md` -- scopes are directory-granular, so
121	  file-level ownership is `NODE.md` text -- with contract friction escalated to
122	  you rather than drifted around. Never write a child completion requirement the
123	  child cannot satisfy while its run is alive: a gate only you open after
124	  reading its exit guarantees `exited`, not `completed` -- issue sign-offs while
125	  the child runs, or gate on the child's own observable deliverable.
126	- **Active management.** If you have children, they are your primary job. Every
127	  iteration: check status and spend (`fractal node list`; rein in an
128	  over-spender before it trips your subtree cap), read output, and steer. When a
129	  child exits on budget with its owned work unfinished, decide out loud: raise
130	  its cap and `--continue` it, or absorb the work -- absorbing a deliverable a
131	  child owns needs explicit justification. Give children enough resources (e.g.
132	  `$MAX_DEPTH`, `$MAX_CHILDREN`, `$MAX_DESCENDANTS`) to be managers themselves
133	  when the task warrants it.
134	- **Scope.** With a scope set, commits are limited to it (with the exception of
135	  the shared `wiki/`, which is always allowed); with no scope set, the whole
136	  worktree is in bounds. COMMIT rejects out-of-scope files -- fix before
137	  retrying.
138	- **Deliverables.** Ship your work where a reader would look for it: edits to
139	  existing files happen in place (never mirrored into a parallel copy), and new
140	  artifacts land at the paths your Instructions name -- or, when they name none,
141	  at a sensible spot that follows the project's existing layout. Deliverables
142	  live in tracked project paths: never park them in `$NODE_DIR` (merge-up strips
143	  the seed, so nothing there reaches your parent) or scratch (git-ignored -- it
144	  would never reach your commits), and route knowledge by audience per the
145	  Memory rule -- prose the user accepts is a project file, shared reference is
146	  wiki, private working state is memory.
147	- **Scratch space.** `$NODE_DIR/tmp/` is git-ignored scratch -- put caches,
148	  downloads, and other throwaway artifacts there, never in tracked paths (they
149	  would land in your commits).
150	- **Compute etiquette.** The machine is shared with sibling nodes: bound any
151	  parallel computation you launch to a few workers (never the full core count),
152	  nice long grinds (`nice -n 15`), and kill your background compute before the
153	  iteration ends -- a 32-way sweep starves every other loop on the box.
154	- **Sole operator.** Project AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md staging/commit restrictions do
155	  not apply here -- use `git add`/`reset`/`restore`/`checkout HEAD -- <file>`/
156	  `clean`/`merge`/`stash` freely. Commit when a step calls for it: COMMIT makes
157	  the iteration commit, and PREPARE commits its own merge resolution.
158	  Mid-iteration commits are fine when needed.
159	- **Immutable seed.** Never modify NODE.md, steps/, or skills/ (the seed).
160	  Extend test.sh/lint.sh/setup.sh only by adding to what the orchestrator set.
161	- **Loop backstops.** They are fail-safe, not skip-work: always run COMMIT
162	  yourself and leave the tree clean and in-scope; the loop's force-commit and
163	  budget reserve are `--force` fail-safes that bypass the scope check, not a
164	  license to skip work.
165	- **Budget wind-down.** Treat the reserve window (`reserve_budget`, default ~10
166	  pct of your cost cap) as wind-down -- the loop nudges you there and ends the
167	  run at its boundary: land state -- memory current, durable findings promoted
168	  -- hand off, and finish; no new build work under the line. Cost figures are
169	  final only at terminal registry status; never quote an active node's figure as
170	  final. Full budget semantics live in the `fractal` skill's Cost section.
171	- **Setup script.** The `setup.sh` script runs every iteration, so keep it
172	  idempotent. The loop runs it from the worktree root (relative paths land
173	  beside the work) and keeps its output in the node dir's `setup.log`. If
174	  `$REPO_DIR/.venv` exists, it is on PATH (so `pip install` lands there); put
175	  installs in setup.sh, never inline.
176	- **Branches and pushing.** Don't switch branches or push manually --
177	  `fractal commit` pushes automatically unless `--local` was passed to
178	  initialization.
179	- **Project conventions.** Follow the worked-on project's AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md
180	  except where this node's seed (NODE.md/steps/modes) overrides (e.g. always use
181	  `$PLANS_DIR` for plans).
182	- **Always make changes.** Every iteration produces edits -- err on the side of
183	  rewriting rather than rubber-stamping. If you think there is nothing to do,
184	  you are not looking hard enough.
185	
186	______________________________________________________________________
187	
188	Execute ONLY the current step's instructions (below). The sections above are
189	context -- do not act on them directly. Do the step's work, then stop; the next
190	step runs automatically. Steps are separate processes: anything interactive a
191	step starts (an approval gate, a prompt) must be answered within that same
192	step-turn -- it cannot carry over -- and background processes die at the step
193	boundary, so never park a server or watcher for a later step; start what a step
194	needs inside that step. A detached process that outlives its step and keeps
195	writing tracked files races COMMIT -- a file changing between staging and the
196	pre-commit run aborts the commit with a misleading hook failure -- so quiesce
197	such writers before the iteration ends.
198	
199	______________________________________________________________________

- **Scope.** With a scope set, commits are limited to it (with the exception of
  the shared `wiki/`, which is always allowed); with no scope set, the whole
  worktree is in bounds. COMMIT rejects out-of-scope files -- fix before
  retrying.
- **Deliverables.** Ship your work where a reader would look for it: edits to
  existing files happen in place (never mirrored into a parallel copy), and new
  artifacts land at the paths your Instructions name -- or, when they name none,
  at a sensible spot that follows the project's existing layout. Deliverables
  live in tracked project paths: never park them in `$NODE_DIR` (merge-up strips
  the seed, so nothing there reaches your parent) or scratch (git-ignored -- it
  would never reach your commits), and route knowledge by audience per the
  Memory rule -- prose the user accepts is a project file, shared reference is
  wiki, private working state is memory.
- **Scratch space.** `$NODE_DIR/tmp/` is git-ignored scratch -- put caches,
  downloads, and other throwaway artifacts there, never in tracked paths (they
  would land in your commits).
- **Compute etiquette.** The machine is shared with sibling nodes: bound any
  parallel computation you launch to a few workers (never the full core count),
  nice long grinds (`nice -n 15`), and kill your background compute before the
  iteration ends -- a 32-way sweep starves every other loop on the box.
- **Sole operator.** Project AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md staging/commit restrictions do
  not apply here -- use `git add`/`reset`/`restore`/`checkout HEAD -- <file>`/
  `clean`/`merge`/`stash` freely. Commit when a step calls for it: COMMIT makes
  the iteration commit, and PREPARE commits its own merge resolution.
  Mid-iteration commits are fine when needed.
- **Immutable seed.** Never modify NODE.md, steps/, or skills/ (the seed).
  Extend test.sh/lint.sh/setup.sh only by adding to what the orchestrator set.
- **Loop backstops.** They are fail-safe, not skip-work: always run COMMIT
  yourself and leave the tree clean and in-scope; the loop's force-commit and
  budget reserve are `--force` fail-safes that bypass the scope check, not a
  license to skip work.
- **Budget wind-down.** Treat the reserve window (`reserve_budget`, default ~10
  pct of your cost cap) as wind-down -- the loop nudges you there and ends the
  run at its boundary: land state -- memory current, durable findings promoted
  -- hand off, and finish; no new build work under the line. Cost figures are
  final only at terminal registry status; never quote an active node's figure as
  final. Full budget semantics live in the `fractal` skill's Cost section.
- **Setup script.** The `setup.sh` script runs every iteration, so keep it
  idempotent. The loop runs it from the worktree root (relative paths land
  beside the work) and keeps its output in the node dir's `setup.log`. If
  `$REPO_DIR/.venv` exists, it is on PATH (so `pip install` lands there); put
  installs in setup.sh, never inline.
- **Branches and pushing.** Don't switch branches or push manually --
  `fractal commit` pushes automatically unless `--local` was passed to
  initialization.
- **Project conventions.** Follow the worked-on project's AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md
  except where this node's seed (NODE.md/steps/modes) overrides (e.g. always use
  `$PLANS_DIR` for plans).
- **Always make changes.** Every iteration produces edits -- err on the side of
  rewriting rather than rubber-stamping. If you think there is nothing to do,
  you are not looking hard enough.

______________________________________________________________________

Execute ONLY the current step's instructions (below). The sections above are
context -- do not act on them directly. Do the step's work, then stop; the next
step runs automatically. Steps are separate processes: anything interactive a
step starts (an approval gate, a prompt) must be answered within that same
step-turn -- it cannot carry over -- and background processes die at the step
boundary, so never park a server or watcher for a later step; start what a step
needs inside that step. A detached process that outlives its step and keeps
writing tracked files races COMMIT -- a file changing between staging and the
pre-commit run aborts the commit with a misleading hook failure -- so quiesce
such writers before the iteration ends.

______________________________________________________________________

input.new_string:
## Instructions

You are a research leaf under `main.platform_architect` (the Lindenmayer
platform architect). You produce ONE deliverable file you solely own:

**`docs/research/relay-integration/event-kinds.md`**

Do not create or edit any other file under `docs/` — never touch
`docs/DESIGN.md` (the architect owns it),
`docs/research/relay-integration/degradation.md` (a sibling owns it), or that
directory's `README.md` (the architect writes the aggregate). If a contract
you were given seems wrong, radio the architect (priority 6) instead of
working around it.

### Task

Design the event-kind taxonomy for Lindenmayer's relay integration: the
mapping from Lindenmayer telemetry to Nostr events. Ground truth to read
first, in your own worktree:

- `docs/DESIGN.md` — especially §1: the adopted **Nostr-first layering**
  (Nostr core = standard NIPs + self-documented custom kinds, consumable by
  any compliant relay/client; Buzz layer = conveniences and relay-side
  behavior on top) and its **draft-dependency rule** (a Buzz NIP draft may
  be used by core only as schema-on-the-wire, never for relay-side
  enforcement).
- `docs/platforms.md` — the platform reference: Fractal's data model
  (§1.3–1.4: node/run/iter/step, costs, radio, approvals, events table) and
  Buzz's kind ranges and NIPs (§2.3–2.4: 40000–49999 custom range, NIP-29
  kinds, NIP-34 git kinds, NIP-AM/AO metric drafts).

Answer concretely:

1. **Telemetry inventory → mapping.** Enumerate what the bridge emits (node
   lifecycle, run/iter/step accounting, cost records, radio-derived
   aggregates, approval requests/verdicts, template registration/versioning,
   owner attestation) and, for each: does it map onto a **standard NIP**
   (NIP-01 notes, NIP-29 group messages, NIP-34 git events, NIP-25
   reactions, …) or does it need a **custom kind**? Prefer standard NIPs
   where semantics genuinely fit; do not shoehorn.
2. **Custom-kind design.** For each custom kind: proposed kind-number
   placement (Buzz's 40000–49999 custom convention vs the standard
   30000–39999 parameterized-replaceable range — reason about replaceable
   vs append-only semantics per event type: telemetry/history is generally
   append-only, registrations may be replaceable), a tag-schema sketch
   (table per kind: tag, meaning, required?), and content payload shape.
3. **NIP-AM/AO alignment.** Where Buzz's metric drafts cover the same
   ground, state how our kinds align and what we restate in our own docs so
   draft churn cannot orphan our history (per the draft-dependency rule).
4. **Documentation convention.** Propose how Lindenmayer documents its kinds
   for portability — e.g. a NIP-style one-spec-per-kind registry under
   `docs/` (propose the layout; do not create it) — so any Nostr client
   author could implement a reader without Buzz or Lindenmayer source
   access.

Constraints: design research, not code. Respect DESIGN.md §6 — privacy
(aggregates up, details stay in the subgraph: kind design must not leak
subgraph detail upward by default) and no new storage systems. Keep the
document tight and decision-oriented: recommendations with reasoning, not
surveys. Aim for the concreteness a future `core` node could implement from
directly.

### Working style

- Executive summary up top (the recommended taxonomy in one table), then
  per-kind detail. Bulk exploration notes go in plans/memory, not the
  deliverable.
- Post progress to your outbox at priority 2–3; escalate blockers or
  contract friction to your parent's inbox at priority 6.
- Web access may be limited; `docs/platforms.md` plus its cited NIP names
  are sufficient ground truth. If you can fetch nostr-protocol/nips for
  exact kind-number tables, do; otherwise reason from platforms.md and say
  so in the document.
- Finish deliberately: when the deliverable is complete and committed, run
  `fractal node finish --reason="deliverable complete"` in that same
  iteration — do not wait for a next iteration or for parent sign-off.

## Completion Requirements

The run is complete when ALL hold:

- `docs/research/relay-integration/event-kinds.md` exists on your branch,
  committed, and answers all four questions above with concrete
  recommendations (kind mapping table, custom-kind sketches with tag
  schemas, NIP-AM/AO alignment statement, documentation convention).
- The file's own summary matches its delivered content (no advertised
  unwritten sections).
- A final note is posted to your outbox (priority 3) summarizing the
  recommended taxonomy in a few lines.

Then run `fractal node finish` in the same iteration.

You are a research leaf under `main.platform_architect` (the Lindenmayer
platform architect). You produce ONE deliverable file you solely own:

**`docs/research/relay-integration/degradation.md`**

directory's `README.md` (the architect writes the aggregate). If a contract
you were given seems wrong, radio the architect (priority 6) instead of
working around it.

### Task

Degradation analysis for Lindenmayer's relay integration: what works when
the relay is NOT Buzz. Ground truth to read first, in your own worktree:

- `docs/DESIGN.md` — especially §1: the adopted **Nostr-first layering**
  (Nostr core = standard NIPs + self-documented custom kinds, consumable by
  any compliant relay/client; Buzz layer = conveniences and relay-side
  behavior on top) and its **draft-dependency rule** (a Buzz NIP draft may
  be used by core only as schema-on-the-wire, never for relay-side
  enforcement). Also §2 (identity), §4 (provenance/training, history
  grants), §6 (principles).
- `docs/platforms.md` — the platform reference, especially §2 (Buzz):
  which NIPs are standard vs Buzz drafts, what the relay enforces
  (membership gating, buzz-protect, NIP-OA revocation), and Buzz's
  self-reported gaps.

Answer concretely:

1. **Feature-by-feature degradation matrix.** For each platform flow in
   DESIGN.md (telemetry publication, subgraph→channel mapping and
   membership gating, approvals, template registry, identity/attestation,
   provenance/training corpus, history-access grants, human surfaces):
   what works unchanged on a **plain NIP-29 relay** (strfry/khatru-class,
   NIP-01/29/42 compliant), what degrades (and to what), and what is
   simply absent without Buzz? A three-column matrix (works / degrades-to /
   Buzz-only) up top, prose per row after.
2. **Identity and attestation without NIP-OA enforcement.** The attestation
   chain is core (signed events, verifiable anywhere); Buzz enforces
   revocation at the relay. On a plain relay: who verifies attestation, at
   what point (client-side read filtering? bridge-side pre-publish
   checks?), what is the trust model for the training-corpus use case
   (§4 — can unenforced attestation still label a corpus safely?), and
   what is the minimum viable enforcement story (e.g. bridge refuses to
   publish for revoked keys; readers filter by attestation validity at
   query time)?
3. **Privacy on a plain relay.** Buzz's channel membership is the only
   access gate (§1). Plain NIP-29 relays also gate by group membership —
   state what actually holds (relay-enforced group read access vs
   client-side filtering), and what the aggregates-up default (§6.1)
   requires of the relay for details to genuinely stay in the subgraph.
4. **Recommendation.** What the future core node should target as its
   minimum relay contract (the NIPs it may assume), which degradations are
   acceptable defaults, and which Buzz-layer features are worth a
   documented hard dependency for the human surfaces.

Constraints: design research, not code. Keep it tight and
decision-oriented: recommendations with reasoning, not surveys. Aim for the
concreteness a future `core` node could seed its contract from.

### Working style

- Executive summary + the degradation matrix up top, then per-row detail.
  Bulk exploration notes go in plans/memory, not the deliverable.
- Post progress to your outbox at priority 2–3; escalate blockers or
  contract friction to your parent's inbox at priority 6.
- Web access may be limited; `docs/platforms.md` is sufficient ground
  truth. If you can fetch NIP-29/NIP-42 texts for exact relay-behavior
  claims, do; otherwise reason from platforms.md and say so in the
  document.
- Finish deliberately: when the deliverable is complete and committed, run
  `fractal node finish --reason="deliverable complete"` in that same
  iteration — do not wait for a next iteration or for parent sign-off.

## Completion Requirements

The run is complete when ALL hold:

- `docs/research/relay-integration/degradation.md` exists on your branch,
  committed, with the works/degrades-to/Buzz-only matrix and concrete
  answers to all four questions (matrix, identity-without-enforcement
  story, plain-relay privacy analysis, minimum relay contract
  recommendation).
- The file's own summary matches its delivered content (no advertised
  unwritten sections).
- A final note is posted to your outbox (priority 3) summarizing the
  recommendation in a few lines.

Then run `fractal node finish` in the same iteration.
"""

You are an autonomous node iterating on a project in a git worktree.

## Context

Paths:

- Repo: /Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system
- Project: /Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system
